narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 65% chance you'll struggle and fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. As a female student from a white background planning to attend full-time with a middle-family income, you stand out with a strong undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—which is a positive note in your profile. However, your school performance tells a different story: you ranked in the bottom decile for grades in both Year 1 and Year 3, far below the average mid-level deciles of around 5.5 to 5.7 for most students. Your LSAT score of 30 is also notably lower than the dataset average of 36.7, placing you behind many peers. When the model weighed all this, your Year 3 decile ranking emerged as the single most influential factor, strongly nudging the prediction toward expecting bar failure. Right behind it, your LSAT score had the next biggest impact, also pushing in the same unfavorable direction. Your Year 1 decile came third in influence, again contributing to the failure outlook, followed by your middle family income bracket exerting a similar pull. Less impactful but still leaning that way were your racial background, undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status—none of which countered the overall trend enough to shift the odds. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, you're on par or better in undergraduate GPA and demographics, but your lower school deciles and LSAT align more closely with those who face first-try failures. To boost your future chances, focus on the areas you can still influence, like retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the 36.7 average, or demonstrating improved academic habits to overcome those early low decile rankings through targeted prep courses. Building stronger study strategies now could help you pass the bar despite the prediction, and reapplying later with evidence of growth might sway admissions. You're already ahead with your undergrad performance—lean into that strength and keep pushing forward.
